content.assessment: Networked retention is the proposal most explicitly aligned with addressing the joint kindergarten-primary coverage gap through hub combination (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08) and with preserving the historically documented segregation-limiting benefit of small non-closed schools (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05, contested). But it does not change the tankerület's exclusive formal authority over school maintenance decisions (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01), meaning cluster formation would still require district-center cooperation or sanction rather than village-level agency, and it must still contend with the same national funding thresholds (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04) that drive closure pressure regardless of network design.
opportunities: The 2026 reform's plan to restore municipal input and principal autonomy could provide new institutional entry points for multi-village network negotiation (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07)
strengths: Explicitly designed to jointly address kindergarten and primary coverage gaps that share the same enrollment-threshold governance logic (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08)
Could preserve the historically documented (contested) segregation-limiting effect of avoiding closures via small classes rather than mergers (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05)
threats: Absent a change to enrollment/efficiency criteria still applied by tankerület centers (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03), individual nodes within any network remain vulnerable to unilateral closure irrespective of cluster-level viability
weaknesses: Does not modify tankerület's exclusive formal authority over closure/maintenance decisions, so network viability remains subject to centralized discretion (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01)
National per-pupil/class-size funding formulas continue to apply regardless of network structure, so fiscal pressure on small nodes within a cluster persists (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04)
